TitleItem: A Systematic Literature Review of Agentic AI Software Frameworks: Architectural Patterns, Orchestration Loops, and Fault-Tolerance Mechanisms in High-Stakes Deployment Environments
AbstractItem: This systematic review synthesizes the current state of agentic AI software frameworks, focusing on orchestration loops, fault-tolerance, and system safety design patterns. Guided by the PRISMA 2020 Expanded Checklist, the study evaluates 16 peer-reviewed sources retrieved from arXiv API, ScienceDirect API, IEEE Xplore API, Google Scholar Gateway, and ResearchGate Gateway using the exact search query 'Agentic AI software frameworks.' The analysis identifies critical architectural components—including perception, planning, action, reflection, and learning stages—and maps their trustworthiness dimensions, particularly safety, robustness, privacy, and system security. Findings reveal that multi-step agentic trajectories amplify failure modes (e.g., prompt injection, tool misuse, and cascading errors) beyond traditional LLM risks, necessitating process-aware evaluation metrics such as constraint violation rates and trace completeness. The review further consolidates mitigation strategies, including runtime safeguards, sandboxing, and adversarial benchmarking, while highlighting open challenges in self-evolving agents and privacy-preserving personalization.
RationaleItem: Agentic AI frameworks represent a paradigm shift from static language models to autonomous systems capable of dynamic planning, tool use, and long-horizon interactions. This evolution introduces novel risks in high-stakes domains such as healthcare, finance, and software engineering, where minor errors in agentic loops can cascade into irreversible real-world consequences. Despite rapid advancements, the literature lacks a unified synthesis of architectural patterns addressing fault-tolerance and safety, particularly in enterprise and multi-agent settings. This review addresses this gap by systematically evaluating frameworks through the lens of trustworthiness, aligning with the growing demand for verifiable, accountable, and resilient AI systems in critical infrastructure.
ObjectivesItem: To evaluate the current state of agentic orchestration loops and identify common design patterns regarding fault-tolerance and system safety.
EligibilityItem: Inclusion criteria required studies to explicitly focus on loop execution and agent architecture, encompassing components such as perception, planning, action, reflection, and learning. Exclusion criteria eliminated studies unrelated to AI frameworks, including agronomy research, commercial marketing materials, and non-technical discussions of agentic systems. Automated screening further filtered for relevance to fault-tolerance, safety mechanisms, or multi-agent coordination, ensuring alignment with the review’s core objective.
SourcesItem: Document searches were conducted exclusively through the following platforms: arXiv API, ScienceDirect API, IEEE Xplore API, Google Scholar Gateway, and ResearchGate Gateway. No additional databases or manual searches were employed.
SearchStrategyItem: The search was executed using the exact phrase 'Agentic AI software frameworks' across all specified databases. No synonyms, Boolean operators, or wildcard variations were applied, ensuring reproducibility and adherence to the zero-hallucination directive. Results were limited to peer-reviewed articles, preprints, and conference proceedings published between 2024 and 2026.
SelectionProcessItem: An automated screening architecture evaluated each document against predefined inclusion and exclusion thresholds. Studies were first filtered for relevance to 'loop execution and agent architecture,' then excluded if they pertained to agronomy or commercial marketing. Metadata and abstracts were parsed to verify alignment with the review’s focus on fault-tolerance and safety, with full-text validation performed for borderline cases. The process was designed to minimize human bias while ensuring strict adherence to the specified constraints.
BiasAssessmentItem: Internal risk of bias is controlled via mandatory post-generation human verification. Because the initial screening and synthesis phases are executed autonomously by an LLM-driven agent framework, all protocol decisions, inclusion metrics, and generated claims require subsequent human oversight, qualitative auditing, and analytical caution prior to formal review deployment.
DiscussionItem: The synthesis highlights a convergence toward modular, workflow-aligned architectures in agentic AI frameworks, where fault-tolerance and safety are embedded at each stage of the orchestration loop. Qi et al. (2026) [2605.23989v1] formalize this approach by mapping risks (e.g., prompt injection, tool misuse) to stage-specific mitigations, such as pre/post-execution safeguards and formal verification of plans. Bandi et al. (2025) [SCHOLAR_a-f_GtGYV9IJ] and Derouiche et al. (2025) [SCHOLAR_QocFfd_Lr1wJ] corroborate these findings, emphasizing constrained optimization and sandboxing as critical for enterprise deployments. Multi-agent systems, as surveyed by Pajo (2025) [RG_5ggDfuX2IoQJ], introduce additional complexity, requiring coordination protocols and ethical design to prevent emergent misalignment. The literature further underscores the tension between autonomy and accountability, with Farooq (2026) [SCOPUS_ID:105040506784] advocating for scenario-to-metric release gating to balance utility and risk. Collectively, these studies suggest that trustworthy agentic AI demands a system-level perspective, integrating technical safeguards with governance frameworks to address cascading failures in high-stakes environments.
SupportItem: This review was supported and conducted within the Department of Engineering & Technology at Aarhus University, funded as part of the IT Vest institutional collaboration framework. The funders played no active role in specific automated screening study designs, live stream extraction cycles, or pipeline synthesis determinations.
AvailabilityItem: All automated retrieval configurations, screening criteria matrices, and synthesis generation pipeline code structures are publicly accessible via the project repository on GitHub at https://github.com/lauPhilip/au-btech-literature-review-agent.
